senses.definition: to make someone feel confident or certain about someone or something

examples.x: The company assured its customers of the product's quality
The doctor assured the patient of the effectiveness of the treatment.
The manager assured the team of the company's commitment to their well-being.
The teacher assured the students of their ability to succeed.
The parent assured the child of their love and support, comforting them during a difficult time.

senses.definition: to express confidence or certainty in something happening or being the case

examples.x: I bet it will rain tomorrow because the sky looks very cloudy.
She bet that her favorite team would win the championship this year.
He's betting that the new restaurant will become a popular spot in town.
The expert bet that the stock prices would rise due to the positive quarterly earnings report.
I bet she's still in bed.

senses.definition: to make sure that something will happen

examples.x: She ensures the quality of the products before they are shipped.
The checklist ensures that all necessary tasks are completed.
The teacher is ensuring that students understand the new lesson.
The chef is ensuring that each dish meets the restaurant's standards.
The captain ensured the safety of the passengers during the storm.

senses.definition: to predict future events, based on analysis of present data and conditions

examples.x: Economists forecast a downturn in the stock market due to recent economic indicators.
The analyst used trends and patterns to forecast an increase in consumer demand.
The software can forecast future trends in user behavior by analyzing historical data.
Meteorologists use advanced models to forecast hurricane paths with increased accuracy.
The financial planner helps clients forecast their future financial needs and goals.

senses.definition: to make sure that something will occur

examples.x: Rigorous testing guarantees that the software will be bug-free before its release to the public.
The thorough training program guarantees that employees will be well-prepared to handle any situation.
Adequate funding guarantees that the project will be completed on time and within budget.

senses.definition: to pause before saying or doing something because of uncertainty or nervousness

examples.x: When asked about the promotion, Sarah hesitated before responding.
Facing the unexpected question, the candidate hesitated for a moment, carefully choosing her words before replying.
John hesitated before entering the unfamiliar building, wondering if he was in the right place.
Confronted with the difficult decision, Tom hesitated, weighing the pros and cons before making a choice.
In the heated debate, the politician hesitated before addressing the controversial topic.

senses.definition: used to talk about what one expects or likes to happen

examples.x: He ought to arrive soon; he said he would be here by noon.
The project ought to be completed by the end of the month, according to the schedule.
They ought to win the game, considering their strong performance in recent matches.
We ought to hear back from them by tomorrow about the job offer.
The repair ought to fix the issue with the leaking faucet.

senses.definition: to bring back something from the memory

examples.x: She could recall the details of the conversation with remarkable clarity.
The professor encouraged students to recall key concepts during the exam.
In therapy, individuals may work to recall suppressed memories for healing.
Last night, he recalled a vivid dream that he had forgotten upon waking.
A scent can often trigger the ability to recall past experiences.

senses.definition: used to indicate a degree of expectation regarding something that is likely to happen

examples.x: You should expect delays during rush hour traffic.
He should receive the package by the end of the week.
The company should announce their decision by tomorrow.
She should pass the exam, considering how well she studied.
We should see improvements in sales after implementing the new marketing strategy.

senses.definition: to think that something is probably true, especially something bad, without having proof

examples.x: I suspect he's been lying about where he was last night.
He didn't say anything, but I suspect he's planning a surprise party for her birthday.
Considering the clouds on the horizon, I suspect it might rain this afternoon.
She suspects there's more to the story than he's telling her.
They suspect the company may be hiding some important information.

senses.definition: used to express an opinion about which one is not certain

examples.x: She would probably agree with your proposal if you explain it clearly.
I would think that they'll arrive before noon, given the traffic conditions.
They would likely support the initiative if it aligns with their values.
He would hope that his hard work will pay off in the end.

senses.definition: likely to happen or sure to experience something
expected or certain to happen

examples.x: You're sure to receive excellent service at that restaurant; they pride themselves on customer satisfaction.
With proper maintenance, the car is sure to run smoothly for years to come.
If you study consistently, you're sure to pass the exam with flying colors.
Given his track record, he's sure to win the championship this year.
With clear skies and good weather, the outdoor event is sure to be a success.

senses.definition: having a strong belief in something

examples.x: He became convinced that his idea would revolutionize the industry.

senses.definition: anticipated or predicted to happen based on previous knowledge or assumptions

examples.x: The students were prepared for the expected test questions after studying the material thoroughly.
The expected arrival time of the train is 3:00 PM, but there may be delays due to track maintenance.
The doctor explained the expected side effects of the medication to the patient before prescribing it.

senses.definition: unable to be prevented

examples.x: As technology advances, it becomes increasingly inevitable that automation will replace certain jobs.
With the heavy rain clouds looming overhead, it seemed inevitable that it would rain soon.
Given the current trajectory of climate change, it's inevitable that sea levels will continue to rise.
The breakup was an inevitable consequence of their constant disagreements.
With tensions escalating between the two countries, war seemed inevitable.

senses.definition: done without carefully considering what might happen

examples.x: He made a rash decision to quit his job without having another one lined up.
The company suffered financial losses due to the CEO's rash spending on unnecessary projects.
They regretted their rash decision to invest all their savings in the volatile stock market.
Selling their house at such a low price was a rash move they later regretted.
His rash purchase of the expensive car left him struggling with debt.

senses.definition: (of a person) having no doubt about something

examples.x: She was positive that she had left her keys on the kitchen counter.
He felt positive that his team would win the championship after their recent victories.
The teacher was positive that her students would excel in the upcoming exam.
Despite the challenges, she remained positive that they would overcome the obstacles together.
The team remained positive despite the setbacks.

senses.definition: having a high possibility of happening or being true based on available evidence or circumstances

examples.x: With clear skies and no signs of storm, it seems probable that the outdoor event will proceed as planned.
The economist predicts that there is a probable increase in inflation rates next quarter.
The detective considered the suspect's alibi to be probable, as it was supported by multiple witnesses.
The survey results suggest that there is a probable decline in consumer confidence in the current economic climate.
The archaeologist believes it's probable that the ancient ruins discovered belong to a previously unknown civilization.

senses.definition: (of a person) showing a lack of confidence and having doubts about something

examples.x: She was uncertain about which job offer to accept, as both had their advantages.
He felt uncertain of his answer on the test, unsure if he had chosen the correct option.
The travelers were uncertain about their route, so they stopped to ask for directions.
They felt uncertain about investing in the new venture without a detailed business plan.

senses.definition: not exactly known or expressed, often leading to confusion or ambiguity

examples.x: It‚Äôs still unclear whether the event will be postponed due to the weather.
His intentions were unclear, making it hard to trust his actions completely.
Their future plans remain unclear as they are still weighing their options.
His intentions were unclear, making it hard to trust his actions completely.

senses.definition: used to say that something is likely to happen or is true

examples.x: The quality of her work was excellent, there's no doubt about it.
He will be late for the meeting, no doubt due to the traffic.
The team's victory was achieved through hard work and determination, no doubt.
The company's success is no doubt attributed to its innovative strategies.
She will win the competition, no doubt about her skills.

senses.definition: the state of being sure about something, usually when there is proof

examples.x: Her certainty in her ability to complete the marathon came from months of dedicated training.
The scientist presented the results with certainty, as the experiments had been thoroughly tested.
Her certainty about the company's financial stability allowed her to invest with confidence.
The doctor‚Äôs certainty in the diagnosis provided comfort and clarity to the patient.
His certainty about the project's success helped persuade others to invest in it.

senses.definition: the belief that one can trust or count on someone or something

examples.x: The coach's confidence in the players motivated them to perform at their best during the game.
The doctor‚Äôs confidence in the treatment plan reassured the patient about their recovery.
His confidence in the product's quality was evident when he spoke about it to potential customers.
The confidence she had in her financial advisor‚Äôs recommendations helped her make important investment decisions.

senses.definition: a state of disorder in which people panic and do not know what to do

examples.x: The sudden loud noise caused confusion among the crowd.
The mix-up with the tickets created confusion at the entrance.
The sudden change in plans caused a lot of confusion among the team.
The sudden blackout resulted in confusion as people stumbled in the dark.
The confusion at the airport was due to canceled flights and long lines.

senses.definition: the likelihood or chance of an event occurring or being true

examples.x: The probability of winning the lottery is extremely low.
There is a high probability of rain tomorrow based on the weather forecast.
The researchers calculated the probability of success for their experiment.
He assessed the probability of passing the exam based on his study habits.
Understanding probability is essential in making informed decisions in gambling and finance.

senses.definition: to make a person believe something untrue

examples.x: He tried to deceive his friends by pretending to be a millionaire.
The magician's tricks were so convincing that they often deceived the audience.
It's unethical to deceive customers with false advertising claims.
She felt hurt when she discovered that her partner had deceived her about their plans.
Online scams aim to deceive people into providing personal information or money.

senses.definition: in a way or by some method that is not known or certain

examples.x: Somehow, she managed to find her way home in the dark.
He lost his keys but somehow got into the house.
The team was behind, but they somehow won the game.
I don't know how, but she somehow completed the task on time.
Despite the obstacles, they somehow made it to the top of the mountain.

senses.definition: a secret plan, particularly one that is made to deceive other people

examples.x: The villain's scheme was to steal the treasure without anyone noticing.
She uncovered a scheme to manipulate the company‚Äôs stock prices.
The detectives investigated the elaborate scheme to defraud investors.
His scheme involved creating fake documents to access restricted areas.
The secret scheme was revealed after months of investigation.

senses.definition: used usually in a response to show that something may not be true

examples.x: Just because someone is wealthy doesn't necessarily mean they're happy.
Expensive restaurants aren‚Äôt necessarily the best.
That is not necessarily true.
Having a high IQ doesn't necessarily guarantee success in life.
Being the eldest in the family doesn't necessarily make you the most responsible.

senses.definition: used to show that someone has made a good suggestion or guess

examples.x: You bet!
You bet, I've got you covered.

senses.definition: to closely examine to see if someone is suitable or something is true

examples.x: The detective will check out the alibi to verify its legitimacy.
I need to check out the new software before recommending it.
The team will check out the equipment to ensure it's in working order.

senses.definition: to fail to keep or fulfill a commitment or assurance made to someone
the likelihood or probability of something actually taking place

examples.x: The odds of winning the grand prize in the raffle are one in a thousand.
She calculated the odds of getting a heads in a single coin toss as 50%.
The doctor explained the odds of recovery, giving the patient hope despite the seriousness of the condition.
Investors weighed the odds of success before deciding to fund the startup.

senses.definition: in a way that is not possible to be changed
to dispose of something by giving or selling it to someone else though persuasion or deception

examples.x: He tried to palm off a fake painting as the real deal.
The salesman palmed off the expired products at a discounted rate without mentioning the dates.
During the garage sale, she managed to palm off all those old magazines to a collector.
I can't believe she palmed off her broken watch on me, saying it was just 'vintage'.

senses.definition: not seeming real, natural, or genuine

word_list.pronunciation: …°Àåo ä bÀà√¶k Àå…ëÀên wÀà ånz wÀà…úÀêd

senses.definition: to fail to keep a promise or commitment that was previously made
to have or express uncertainty about something

examples.x: She questioned the validity of the research findings due to inconsistencies in the data.
The students questioned the accuracy of the textbook's information and sought additional sources for verification.
The voters questioned the candidate's promises, wanting more concrete plans for implementation.
They questioned the motives behind the sudden change in policy, suspecting hidden agendas.
She questioned her own judgment after making a mistake and sought feedback from colleagues.
